description: This Prudential Standard applies to all ADIs except foreign ADIs and purchased payment facility providers, on both Level 1 and Level 2 bases.
Where an ADI is the holding company for a group, it must ensure requirements are met on a Level 2 basis. Where an ADI is a subsidiary of an authorised NOHC, the NOHC must ensure Level 2 compliance.
ADIs must apply this Prudential Standard to calculate RWA for credit exposures of overseas banking subsidiaries forming part of the Level 2 group.

For Level 2 Regulatory Capital requirements for credit exposures of overseas banking subsidiaries regulated by a prescribed New Zealand authority, an ADI must calculate RWA using the prescribed New Zealand authority's equivalent prudential rules.
An ADI must hold Regulatory Capital commensurate with its credit risk exposure, applying specified risk weights and CCFs, and using ECAI ratings where appropriate.
An ADI must establish and implement effective internal policies, processes, systems and controls to ensure appropriate risk weights are assigned to its credit exposures.

If an ADI seeks to use LMI or eligible CRM techniques to reduce Regulatory Capital requirements, it must have effective policies, procedures and systems in place, and must address residual risks including legal, operational, liquidity and market risks.

An ADI must calculate RWA for on-balance sheet exposures by multiplying the current book value (including accrued interest or revaluations, net of provisions, write-offs and depreciation) by the relevant risk weight.
RWA for non-SFT off-balance sheet exposures must be calculated using a two-step process: first determining EAD per Attachment C or APS 180, then multiplying by the relevant risk weight.

For SFTs, an ADI must calculate RWA for counterparty credit risk (EAD per Attachment G multiplied by relevant risk weight) and separately calculate capital for credit or market risk in securities lent or posted as collateral where that risk remains with the ADI.

Where multiple CRM techniques cover a single exposure, the ADI must divide the exposure into separately calculated portions and total the resulting RWA. Credit protection from a single provider with differing maturities must also be subdivided.
An ADI must not recognise additional CRM on exposures where the risk weight is mapped from an ECAI issue-specific credit rating that already incorporates CRM recognition.
An ADI must not recognise CRM where the credit quality of the counterparty has a material positive correlation with the CRM technique used or any resulting residual risks.

To obtain capital relief through CRM, all documentation must be legally binding on all parties and enforceable in all relevant jurisdictions, with sufficient legal review undertaken and periodic reviews to confirm ongoing enforceability.
Property exposures must be risk-weighted according to Attachment A requirements, except defaulted property exposures which must be risk-weighted under Attachment E.
A loan must meet all requirements in paragraphs 3 to 7 of Attachment A to be classified as a standard loan.

An ADI must have unequivocal enforcement rights over mortgaged property at all times, including right to possession and power of sale on default, and must reasonably consider it can realise the property's value within a reasonable timeframe.

An ADI's property exposure must be secured by a registered first mortgage, or a registered second mortgage meeting specific conditions including written consent of first mortgagee, restrictions on increasing the first mortgage amount, independent power of sale, and fair market value requirements on sale.

Prior to loan approval, an ADI must document, assess and verify the borrower's ability to meet repayment obligations, including assessment of higher interest rate impacts, all existing debt commitments, serviceability for interest-only residential loans on P&I terms, and tenancy profile for cash-flow-dependent commercial loans. Loans failing this assessment must be classified as non-standard.

An ADI must appropriately value any property offered as security per paragraph 11 of Attachment A, and except for ADC exposures, the property value must not depend materially on the borrower's performance.
All information required at loan origination and for monitoring must be accurately documented and readily accessible, including borrower repayment ability and property valuation information.

Property exposures originated prior to 1 January 2023 with no material change to loan terms and conditions after that date do not need to satisfy the serviceability assessment criteria in paragraph 5 to be classified as standard loans.

LVR components must be prudently calculated per paragraphs 10 and 11 of Attachment A. The loan amount includes outstanding balance (including accrued interest and fees) and any undrawn committed amount, calculated gross of provisions and credit risk mitigants.

Where an ADI provides multiple sequential-ranking loans secured by the same property, the loan amounts must be aggregated and treated as a single exposure for LVR calculation. Where a second mortgage is provided, the LVR must be calculated as the sum of all claims secured by both first and second mortgages.

ADIs must maintain the property value at origination for LVR purposes unless specific exceptions apply (new valuation, permanent value reduction, or confirmed value-increasing modifications). Where a loan is secured by both residential and commercial properties, a 40 per cent haircut must be applied to the commercial property value when calculating LVR.
For reverse mortgages where a borrower is guaranteed a minimum share of future sale proceeds, the LVR calculation must be adjusted by reducing the property value by the quarantined percentage share.
For commercial property exposures originated prior to 1 January 2023, an ADI may use the current valuation rather than the origination value for LVR purposes.
An ADI may use eligible CRM techniques to reduce the exposure amount of a property exposure, but the LVR band and applicable risk weight must be determined before applying any CRM technique.

Where a loan has more than one purpose, the entire loan must be classified according to the predominant purpose. Where there is any doubt about whether a loan is owner-occupied or investment, it must be treated as 'other standard residential property'.

An ADI may reduce its capital requirement through LMI only where the insurance covers all losses up to at least 40 per cent of the higher of the original or outstanding loan amount and is provided by an eligible insurer regulated by APRA (Level 1) or an overseas prudential regulator (Level 2).
ADIs must apply the risk weights in Table 1 to standard residential property exposures based on loan classification (owner-occupied P&I or other standard), LMI status, and LVR band.

An ADI may apply a risk weight of 35 per cent to residential property exposures qualifying under the Australian Government's Home Guarantee Scheme where the National Housing Finance and Investment Corporation has issued a guarantee certificate.

Exposures secured by residential property that do not meet standard loan requirements (paras 3-7) must be classified as non-standard loans. Specific loan types including interest-only loans with LVR above 80 per cent and term over five years, reverse mortgages, shared equity mortgages, and SMSF loans must also be classified as non-standard.

ADIs must apply the risk weights in Table 2 to non-standard residential property loans based on LVR, with reverse mortgages attracting 50 per cent (LVR ≤ 60%) or 100 per cent (LVR > 60%) and all other non-standard loans attracting 100 per cent.
A non-standard residential loan that failed only the para 5 criteria may be reclassified as standard where the loan has been performing consecutively for the previous 36 months.
Where repayment of a commercial property exposure depends primarily on property cash flows, ADIs must apply the risk weights in Table 3 based on LVR and standard/non-standard classification.

Where repayment of a commercial property exposure does not depend primarily on property cash flows, ADIs must apply the risk weights in Table 4 based on counterparty type, LVR, and standard/non-standard classification.

To classify a commercial property exposure as 'not dependent on property cash flows', the ADI must have recourse to a borrower meeting all criteria including being a corporate entity with greater than $250 million in tangible assets, no single real estate asset exceeding 25 per cent of portfolio value, and tenant diversification requirements.

All ADC exposures where the predominant property security is not fully completed must be classified as ADC unless the property is for forest/agricultural purposes or is a residential property under construction that will be the borrower's primary residence.

An ADI may apply a 100 per cent risk weight to ADC exposures secured by residential property where conditions are met including total debt to qualifying development costs below 75 per cent and, for exposures above $5 million, pre-sales of at least 100 per cent of total debt.
ADIs must apply a risk weight of 150 per cent to all ADC exposures that do not qualify for the 100 per cent risk weight treatment.
ADIs must risk-weight all non-property exposures in accordance with Attachment B, except for unsettled/failed transactions (Attachment D) and defaulted exposures (Attachment E).

ADIs must apply the risk weights in Table 5 to sovereign exposures based on external credit rating. Exposures to the Australian Government and RBA denominated and funded in Australian dollars may be risk-weighted at zero per cent.
ADIs must apply the risk weights in Table 6 to domestic PSE exposures that do not qualify as sovereign exposures, based on external credit rating.

ADIs must apply the risk weights in Table 7 to bank exposures based on external credit rating and maturity. Short-term treatment is available for exposures with original maturity of three months or less, or trade-related exposures of six months or less.

Unrated bank exposures must be risk-weighted at the higher of the standard floor (20 per cent short-term, 50 per cent long-term) or the risk weight applicable to the sovereign of the bank's country of incorporation.
ADIs must apply Table 8 risk weights to short-term bank exposures with issue-specific external credit ratings, and must not use ratings that incorporate assumptions of implicit government support.

ADIs may apply the reduced risk weights in Table 9 to rated covered bond exposures only where the cover pool meets specified asset eligibility criteria, the cover pool exceeds the outstanding covered bond value by at least 10 per cent, and these conditions are maintained throughout the bond's maturity.

ADIs must be able to demonstrate to APRA that cover pool information including value, geographical distribution, asset type, loan size, interest rate and currency risks is available on at least a semi-annual basis.
Unrated covered bonds or those not meeting paragraphs 15-16 requirements must be treated as bank exposures and risk-weighted using Table 7.
ADIs must apply risk weights from Table 10 to general corporate exposures with an external long-term credit rating.
ADIs must apply risk weights from Table 11 to short-term general corporate exposures with an issue-specific external credit rating.
Where a short-term issue-specific rating maps to grade 4, all unrated exposures to that counterparty must be risk-weighted at 150%.

An SME exposure is defined as one to a corporate counterparty with total consolidated annual revenue below $75 million; where revenue is undeterminable and the exposure is below $5 million, the ADI may treat it as SME.
ADIs must apply risk weights from Table 12 to unrated SME exposures: 75% for SME retail and 85% for SME corporate.
To qualify as SME retail, the exposure must be a small business lending facility or commitment and the maximum aggregated exposure to the SME counterparty must be less than $1.5 million.

For unrated general corporate exposures not treated as SME, ADIs must apply 100% risk weight to all exposures, or 85% to investment grade and 110% to non-investment grade under an APRA-approved methodology.
ADIs must apply risk weights from Table 13 to unrated specialised lending exposures: 110% for project finance and 100% for object and commodities finance.
ADIs must apply risk weights from Table 14 to retail exposures: 75% for credit card exposures and 100% for other retail exposures.
Margin lending exposures secured by eligible financial collateral must be risk-weighted at 20%; those secured by other collateral must be risk-weighted at 100%.
Subordinated debt not required to be deducted from regulatory capital under APS 111 must be risk-weighted at 150%.
Equity exposures not deducted from Regulatory Capital must be risk-weighted at 250% if listed on a recognised exchange (or to banking/insurance subsidiaries at Level 1) and 400% if unlisted.

Where an ADI bears residual value risk in operating leases, it must apply 100% risk weight to the portion of aggregate residual value up to 10% of Tier 1 capital and 250% to the portion exceeding 10% of Tier 1 capital.

ADIs must apply a 150% risk weight to credit exposures originated through a third party where the ADI did not conduct credit assessment, cannot administer workout/default processes, and has no direct recourse.
ADIs must apply risk weights from Table 16 to all other exposures not classified as property or other specified asset classes.

For exposures originated after 1 January 2023, ADIs must apply a 1.5x multiplier to the applicable risk weight for unhedged retail or residential property exposures to individuals where the lending currency differs from the borrower's income currency.
An ADI may only treat an exposure as hedged for currency mismatch purposes where the natural or financial hedge covers at least 90% of the loan repayment amount.
Where a commitment exists, ADIs must multiply the committed but undrawn amount by the relevant CCF in Table 17 and then risk-weight the exposure according to Attachments A, B and D.
Where an ADI has given a commitment to provide an off-balance sheet exposure, it may apply the lower of the CCFs applicable to the commitment and the off-balance sheet exposure.
ADIs must apply escalating risk weights from Table 18 to DvP transactions remaining unsettled after their due delivery dates, based on the number of business days elapsed.

ADIs must hold Regulatory Capital against non-DvP transactions where payment or delivery has been made without receipt, applying a 1250% risk weight from five business days after payment/delivery until extinction of the transaction.

For equity exposures, ADIs must apply risk weights based on the counterparty as per Attachment B, or apply a 100 per cent risk weight for non-material exposures provided all such exposures are treated consistently.
Where an exposure is in default, an ADI must apply the risk weights in Table 19 or Table 20 to the exposure amount, net of any provisions for defaulted exposures and partial write-offs.
An ADI must apply the risk weights in Table 19 to its defaulted residential property exposures, with risk weights varying by loan type and LMI status.

An ADI must apply the risk weights in Table 20 to defaulted exposures that are not residential property exposures, with risk weights varying by the level of provisions relative to the outstanding exposure amount.
An ADI may only use solicited ECAI ratings to determine credit rating grades for risk-weighting purposes, and must use ratings consistently for each type of exposure.
An ADI must only use an ECAI rating that reflects the entire amount of credit risk exposure the ADI has with regard to all amounts owed to it.

An ADI must perform due diligence on external ratings; if due diligence reflects higher risk than the external rating implies, the ADI must assign a risk weight at least one bucket higher than that determined by the external rating.
An ADI must not use credit ratings for one entity within a corporate group to determine the risk weight for other entities within the same group.

Where a counterparty has multiple ECAI ratings corresponding to different credit rating grades, an ADI must apply the higher risk weight where there are two ratings, or the higher of the two lowest risk weights where there are three or more ratings.
An ADI must not use an ECAI rating denominated in one currency to derive the credit rating grade for an exposure denominated in another currency to the same counterparty.

An ADI must select either the simple approach or the comprehensive approach for collateralised transactions and apply it consistently to all banking book exposures for which it has received collateral. An ADI approved to use the internal ratings-based approach to credit risk is not permitted to use the simple approach.
For trading book exposures, an ADI must use the comprehensive approach where it has received collateral from the counterparty.

There must be a contract establishing the ADI's direct, explicit, irrevocable and unconditional recourse to received collateral; a common law right of set-off alone is insufficient for cash collateral.
An ADI must take all steps necessary to obtain and maintain an enforceable security interest in received collateral and have clear procedures for timely liquidation of collateral.
Where posted or received collateral is held by a custodian or third party, the ADI must take reasonable steps to ensure the custodian or third party segregates the collateral from other assets.

An ADI must have collateral risk management policies in place to control, monitor and report on margin agreement risks, concentration risk, collateral re-use, and surrender of rights on posted collateral.

Under the simple approach, received collateral must be pledged for at least the life of the exposure, marked-to-market and revalued with a minimum frequency of six months, and its release must be conditional upon repayment of the exposure.

Under the simple approach, the risk weight on the collateral-secured portion of an exposure is subject to a floor of 20 per cent, except under conditions specified in paragraphs 18 to 20; the unsecured portion must be risk-weighted according to the original counterparty's risk weight.

A zero per cent risk weight may be applied to collateralised transactions where the exposure and collateral are in the same currency and the collateral is either cash on deposit or sovereign securities eligible for zero per cent risk weight discounted by 20 per cent.
SFTs meeting specified conditions may be risk-weighted at 10 per cent, or at zero per cent where the counterparty is a core market participant as defined in paragraph 20.

Under the simple approach, eligible financial collateral is limited to specified items including cash collateral, gold bullion, rated debt securities meeting minimum credit rating grade thresholds, unrated bank senior debt meeting specified conditions, senior securitisation exposures with credit rating grade of one, and qualifying trust units.
Resecuritisation exposures as defined in APS 120 are not eligible financial collateral under the simple approach.

Under the comprehensive approach, eligible financial collateral includes items eligible under the simple approach plus equities in main indices or listed on recognised exchanges, and qualifying trust units investing in those items.
For collateralised transactions under the comprehensive approach including SFTs, an ADI must adjust EAD using the specified formula incorporating haircuts for collateral and foreign exchange.
Where a maturity mismatch exists, collateral may only be recognised if the original maturity of the term of lodgement is at least 12 months and the residual maturity is at least 3 months.

Where a maturity mismatch exists, an ADI must apply the prescribed formula to adjust the collateral value, using the minimum of the residual collateral maturity and the exposure maturity (capped at 5 years).
An ADI must use the supervisory haircuts set out in Table 23 to determine haircuts for collateral and exposure items.
An ADI must apply a haircut of 8 per cent for currency risk where the exposure and collateral are denominated in different currencies, and zero per cent where they are in the same currency.
An ADI must apply the minimum holding periods and conditions in Table 24 to SFTs, other capital-market-driven transactions and secured lending.
For netting sets where the number of trades exceeds 5,000 at any point during a quarter, an ADI must set the minimum holding period to 20 business days for the following quarter.
For netting sets containing illiquid collateral or OTC derivatives that cannot be easily replaced, an ADI must set the minimum holding period to 20 business days.

If an ADI has experienced more than two margin call disputes on a netting set over the previous two quarters lasting longer than the applicable minimum holding period, it must use a minimum holding period at least double the supervisory floor for that netting set for the subsequent two quarters.
An ADI must calculate the margining frequency adjustment factor using the prescribed formula based on the minimum holding period and the number of business days between remarginings or revaluations.
For SFTs covered by an eligible bilateral netting agreement, an ADI using the comprehensive approach must calculate EAD using the prescribed formula incorporating net and gross exposures.
An ADI that chooses to net transactions must do so on a consistent basis and must apply netting to all transactions in both the banking book and trading book covered by a netting agreement.

An ADI that applies netting for Regulatory Capital purposes must have a netting policy that addresses the requirements in this Prudential Standard, including systems and controls for monitoring and reporting netted transactions and managing roll-off and termination risk.
An ADI must maintain adequate records to support the application of the netting agreement.
For Regulatory Capital purposes, an ADI may only net on-balance sheet loans and deposits, SFTs, and OTC derivative transactions with a single counterparty (but not across derivatives and SFTs).

An ADI may only net internal transactions between the banking book and trading book if the netted transactions are marked-to-market daily and any collateralised instruments comply with eligible collateral criteria in Attachment G.

An ADI may only use net exposures of loans and deposits as the basis for its RWA calculation where it satisfies all four conditions in paragraph 7, including legal enforceability, ability to identify netted positions, and monitoring of roll-off and net exposures.

An ADI must obtain a written and reasoned legal opinion confirming the enforceability of a netting agreement in all relevant jurisdictions, and must have policies to keep legal enforceability under review.

An ADI must not recognise a netting agreement for Regulatory Capital purposes if there is any doubt as to its legal enforceability, including where a foreign regulator has indicated netting is not enforceable under its laws.

Where an exposure is covered by a guarantee from an eligible guarantor, an ADI may substitute the risk weight of the counterparty for the risk weight of the guarantor for the covered portion; the uncovered portion must be risk-weighted at the original counterparty's risk weight.

To be recognised as eligible CRM, a guarantee must satisfy all minimum requirements in paragraph 2, including being a direct, explicitly documented, irrevocable, unconditional claim on an eligible guarantor covering all expected payments.

An ADI must only recognise guarantees from sovereigns, banks, externally rated entities (for non-securitisation exposures), or entities with credit rating grade three or better (for securitisation exposures), and only where the guarantor has a lower risk weight than the counterparty.
For securitisation exposures, eligible guarantors must have a credit rating grade of three or better and must have been externally rated two or better at the time the credit protection was provided.

An indirect guarantee by the Australian Government may be treated as a direct government guarantee only if it covers all credit risk elements, meets all paragraph 2 requirements (except directness), and the ADI is satisfied the coverage is robust.
Letters of comfort do not qualify as eligible guarantees for CRM purposes.

Where a guarantee includes a materiality threshold on payments below which no payment will be made in the event of loss, it is equivalent to a retained first loss position and must be deducted from Common Equity Tier 1 Capital in accordance with APS 111, capped at the capital required against the full exposure.
Where there is partial coverage of equal seniority between the ADI and guarantor (pro rata loss sharing), capital relief is afforded on a proportional basis.

Where a guarantee has a currency mismatch, an ADI must reduce the protected amount by applying an 8 per cent haircut based on a 10 business day holding period and daily marking-to-market, adjusted for actual revaluation frequency.
A guarantee with a maturity mismatch may only be recognised as eligible CRM if the original maturity is at least 12 months and the residual maturity is at least 3 months.

Where a single guarantor provides credit protection with different maturities to the same underlying exposure, an ADI must divide the exposure into separate covered portions for risk-weighting purposes.
Where there is a maturity mismatch on a guarantee, an ADI must apply the prescribed adjustment formula P_a = P × (t − 0.25) / (T − 0.25) to determine the adjusted guarantee value.

An ADI purchasing credit protection through an eligible credit derivative may substitute the risk weight of the counterparty with that of the credit protection seller for the covered portion; the uncovered portion retains the original counterparty risk weight.

Eligible credit derivatives for CRM purposes are limited to single-name credit-default swaps and certain total-rate-of-return swaps; total-rate-of-return swaps where net payments are recorded as income without offsetting asset value deterioration must not be recognised.

To be recognised as eligible CRM, a credit derivative must meet all minimum requirements including being a direct claim, explicitly referenced, irrevocable, unconditional, specifying required credit events, not terminating before grace periods expire, and clearly identifying parties responsible for determining credit events.

Where a credit derivative is based on cash settlement, the ADI must have a robust valuation process to reliably estimate losses on the reference obligation, including a defined period for obtaining post-credit-event valuations.

If restructuring is excluded from the credit derivative contract but all other minimum requirements are met, an ADI may only recognise 60 per cent of the lesser of the credit protection amount and the underlying exposure amount.

An ADI must only recognise credit protection from eligible providers: sovereigns, banks, externally rated entities for non-securitisation exposures, and for securitisation exposures, entities with a credit rating grade of three or better (and rated two or better at time of provision).

Where a credit derivative includes a materiality threshold below which no payment is made in the event of loss, it must be treated as a retained first loss position and deducted from Common Equity Tier 1 Capital in accordance with APS 111, capped at the capital required for the full exposure.

Where a credit derivative provides partial coverage with equal seniority between protected and unprotected portions, capital relief is afforded on a proportional basis; where there is a difference in seniority, the arrangement is treated as a synthetic securitisation subject to APS 120.

Where a credit derivative used as CRM has a currency mismatch, the ADI must apply an 8 per cent haircut (based on a 10 business day holding period and daily marking-to-market), adjusted for actual revaluation frequency per Attachment G paragraph 32.
A credit derivative with a maturity mismatch may only be recognised as eligible CRM if the original maturity is at least 12 months and the residual maturity is at least 3 months.
Where there is a maturity mismatch on a credit derivative, an ADI must apply the prescribed adjustment formula P_a = P × (t − 0.25) / (T − 0.25) to determine the adjusted credit protection value.

An ADI selling credit protection not covered by paragraphs 29-30 must undertake a written assessment of Regulatory Capital treatment prior to execution, notify APRA before execution, and apply that treatment unless APRA determines otherwise.
Total-rate-of-return swaps used to hedge banking book credit exposures must be included in the banking book; all other total-rate-of-return swaps must be included in the trading book.
Any instruments giving rise to a net short credit or equity position in the banking book must be included in the ADI's trading book.

An ADI that has sold credit protection using a credit derivative must assume that 100 per cent of the credit risk is purchased for capital adequacy purposes, irrespective of the range of specified credit events.

An ADI selling credit protection via a credit derivative with an embedded option to extend the term must assume the longest possible effective maturity, regardless of contractual incentives to reduce the term.
When determining the amount of credit protection sold, an ADI must assume that any materiality thresholds in the credit derivative contract do not reduce the acquired credit risk.

For credit protection sold via a credit-default swap on a single reference entity, the ADI must apply the risk weight applicable to the reference entity, with the exposure amount equal to the maximum possible amount payable under the contract if a credit event occurs.

For first-to-default basket credit derivatives, risk weights of all basket assets must be aggregated up to a maximum of 1250 per cent and multiplied by the nominal protection amount to obtain the RWA; for second-to-default, the asset with the lowest risk-weighted amount may be excluded, with analogous treatment for nth-to-default.
